notes.text: In fact, there are two (isomorphic) ways to approach orthogonal groups. Via matrices (which is what the Lean snippet is about) and via Euclidean spaces, or more generally spaces with a symmetric bilinear form (which is what the wikidata QID is about. I'm not sure what the status of the latter approach is in Mathlib, but it would be best to tag that. Or maybe both.

triage.fix_hint: Drop; only Matrix.orthogonalGroup and BilinForm.orthogonal (a subspace) exist.

notes.text: This is just the map itself, but says nothing about the iteration behavior / method of finding roots. We want something more like Wikipedia's "Proof of quadratic convergence for Newton's iterative method"

triage.reason: Reviewer wants the quadratic-convergence theorem for Newton's method, not the map itself; Mathlib's Dynamics/Newton.lean defines the iteration map but does not contain a clean convergence theorem suitable as the Q374195 target.

notes.text: This does not cover polar/spherical/hyperbolic coordinate systems as discussed in the Wikipedia article. "Coordinate system" is a much more general concept. Instead, we should tag `Basis` with Q189569 (Basis)

triage.reason: Q11210 (Coordinate system) is too general for any single Mathlib declaration; reviewer's suggested retarget (Basis ↔ Q189569) is a different QID and belongs in a fresh tag, not a fix to this one.

notes.text: Do we also want to tag `Quotient` or `Quot`? Those are technically in Core, not Mathlib, but are the true type-theoretic foundations of equiv classes.
I think this is fine. But we could add another `attribute` command below this def, that tags those.

I am not really convinced about this. The "true" equivalent of the set of equivalence classes using DTT is that of Setoids, I think, and having sets-of-sets here is a way around the fact that our basic objects are types and not sets.

triage.reason: Reviewers split on whether equivalence classes in DTT correspond to Setoid (faenuccio) or Quot/Quotient (Deicyde) rather than Setoid.classes, with no consensus on a single correct retarget.

notes.text: Not sure: the wikimedia thing is much more specialised than the Mathlib thing, and it's intricate to pass from one to another.

This time wikipedia is much more general than Mathlib.

notes.text: True, but given the ubiquitous use of order theory in Mathlib, I would avoid connecting it to something that is inherently "C^2".

triage.reason: Reviewer faenuccio objects to connecting an order-theoretic Mathlib definition to a Wikidata concept rooted in C² analysis, and no more-specific order-theoretic QID for saddle points exists.

Q582659 ('Exponential growth', elementary concept x_t = x_0(1+r)^t) is fundamentally too broad for ExpGrowth.expGrowthSup, which is a specific asymptotic limsup-of-growth-exponents construction in Mathlib's Asymptotics library — no reviewer note points toward a fixable retarget, and no Wikidata QID for this precise asymptotic object exists.

notes.text: Already exists. I will fix the script.

triage.reason: Reviewer notes the tag already exists in Mathlib; this is a duplicate produced by the tagging script, not a wrong declaration or wrong QID, so there is nothing to fix by requeuing.

notes.text: Also make sure we are tagging the **complex** roots of unity, since that is what is described in the Wikidata item

triage.reason: The reviewer's only stated concern (ensuring the declaration targets the complex case) is already satisfied — Complex.mem_rootsOfUnity lives in Mathlib.RingTheory.RootsOfUnity.Complex and Q756747's own description reads 'a complex number whose positive integer power equals one' — leaving no concrete change to apply, making a valid requeue impossible under the must-change-something rule.

Q163875 ('Cardinal number') is the broader parent of Q4049983 ('Cardinality'), which is already correctly tagged on `Cardinal`; no specific retargeting is possible without creating a redundant or too-broad tag on the same declaration.

The mapping is clean and correct — Q159375 (variance) maps directly to ProbabilityTheory.variance (the primary def) — but no reviewer ever voted or noted any issue, so there is nothing to fix; requeue requires at least one change, leaving cut as the only valid option; the tag can be resubmitted as a fresh high-confidence proposal.

No reviewer notes identify any fixable mismatch, and while the mapping of `Cardinal` (an equivalence class of types under bijection) to Q4049983 (Cardinality) is plausible, the Mathlib type formalizes *cardinal numbers* as mathematical objects rather than the abstract *property* of cardinality, suggesting a more specific QID (e.g. for 'cardinal number') may be warranted — better resolved via a fresh, QID-verified submission than a blind requeue.

Q186475 (partial derivative) is the analytic/calculus concept, but MvPolynomial.pderiv formalizes the purely algebraic formal polynomial derivative as a bundled Derivation morphism — a structural QID mismatch with no reviewer-supplied fix target.

notes.text: Close but not quite. A Borel measure is a measure defined on a `BorelSpace`, which I'm not sure is explicitly defined in Mathlib

triage.reason: Q892585 is Borel measure while BorelSpace formalizes the underlying measurable space structure; no clean replacement QID for the general 'Borel space' concept (distinct from the more restrictive 'standard Borel space') can be identified, and the reviewer flagged uncertainty about Mathlib coverage of Borel measure itself.

notes.text: Mathlib is a strict generalization. It does not require the distribution to be continuous.

triage.reason: MeasureTheory.pdf.IsUniform is parametric over any reference measure μ on any measurable space (discrete counting measure works just as well as Lebesgue), so it strictly generalizes Q274506's continuous uniform distribution on a real interval, and no continuous-only Mathlib declaration exists in the file to retarget the tag to.

notes.text: This is the internal of the itauto tactic, and not considered as public API of Mathlib, right? I don't think we should tag anything in the Tactic folder

triage.reason: Both reviewers rejected on categorical grounds — Mathlib.Tactic.ITauto.IProp is an internal tactic implementation type, not a formalized mathematical concept, and neither reviewer suggested a concrete alternative declaration to retarget to.
